message.content: <local-command-caveat>Caveat: The messages below were generated by the user while running local commands. DO NOT respond to these messages or otherwise consider them in your response unless the user explicitly asks you to.</local-command-caveat>
<local-command-stdout>Set model to [1mFable 5[22m and saved as your default for new sessions</local-command-stdout>
Look through this app and see if there is anything we can do to improve it

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- code-review: Reviews the current branch diff and all staged, unstaged, and untracked files for correctness bugs and quality issues before a commit, holding each file to the best practices of the language and framework it belongs to (Java 25 / Micronaut with GraalVM native-image, plain JDBC / Postgres, Go, and vanilla ES6 JavaScript). Use before every commit, and again before opening a PR only if the reviewed state changed. Reports findings; does not edit. (Tools: Bash, Read, Grep, Glob)

- entire-search: Search Entire checkpoint history and transcripts with `entire search --json`. Use proactively when the user asks about previous work, commits, sessions, prompts, or historical context in this repository. (Tools: Bash)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

- ui-review: Expert reviewer of command-line tools and websites, grounded in established best-practice standards for both. This project ships both a CLI (agent-ingest) and a web UI, so review whichever the branch touches. Use after an implementation pass that touches a CLI or the web UI, before verification, to review the branch diff against CLI conventions (arguments, help and error output, exit codes, stdio, composability) and web standards (semantic HTML, WCAG accessibility, performance, consistency). (Tools: Bash, Read, Grep, Glob)

- verifier: Runs the builds and tests appropriate to a change and reports the results. Use after implementation (and after ui-review) to confirm the change is sound before code review. Reports failures, flakes, missing coverage, and environment issues; does not fix them. (Tools: Bash, Read, Grep, Glob)

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact page can be granted — behavior static HTML cannot provide on its own, such as the page reading live or connected data, keeping state shared across viewers, or updating and republishing itself. Serves this user's live capability roster and the typed call definitions. Load it whenever the user asks for an artifact needing any such runtime behavior.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
# Agent Brain Visualizer

## What is this project? The Agent Brain Visualizer is a dedicated companion tool for developers working with AI coding agents. Agents construct complex reasoning chains, dispatch background tasks, spawn subagents, and execute system commands over long-running sessions, recording all of these interactions in detailed JSONL transcript files (the agent's "brain"). It reads sessions from Antigravity (CLI/IDE/Agent), OpenAI Codex, and Claude Code. This visualizer parses those raw JSONL brain transcripts and renders them in a scannable and interactive web interface, allowing developers to inspect the agent's exact decision-making process. ## How it works The visualizer reads agent sessions from a shared [Postgres store](#the-trajectory-store--postgres). Trajectories get there by being **pushed**: the [`agent-ingest`](cli/README.md) CLI scans a machine's local transcripts (Antigravity, OpenAI Codex, Claude Code) and uploads them to the app, which normalizes and stores them. So a session captured on one computer is visible from any machine pointed at the same store. When you select a session from the sidebar, the application renders its normalized steps into interactive sequences. Additionally, it leverages Google's Gemini LLMs to automatically generate comprehensive executive summaries of long conversations—distilling thousands of lines of transcript into the core user intent, key technical decisions, and the final outcome of the session. These summaries are cached in the store, so a summary generated on one machine is available on the others. ### Key Features **Session Management** * **Session Loading**: Lists the ingested sessions for the selected source from the store, newest first. * **Multiple Sources**: Antigravity (`antigravity-cli` / `antigravity-ide`), **OpenAI Codex**, and **Claude Code** sessions are all ingested into the same store and rendered in one timeline view. The `agent-ingest` CLI locates each tool's transcripts (`~/.gemini`, `~/.codex/sessions`, `~/.claude/projects`) and pushes them; the server normalizes each tool's format into the shared step schema and caches AI summaries alongside them. > **Note:** Inline file preview is available for Antigravity transcripts, which embed explicit file references. Codex and Claude Code sessions reference files through shell/tool calls rather than the structured `file://` links Antigravity emits, so clickable file previews are not generated for them. * **Fleet Insights**: A **📊 Insights** button opens a cross-session dashboard for the selected source — session/outcome counts, busiest tools, the most common errors, average tool calls and duration, and a rolled-up backlog of the recommendations and issues surfaced by the per-session AI analyses. Every tally row is **drill-down**: click it to see (and open) the sessions behind it. * **Skill / AGENTS.md Miner**: A **🛠️ Mine** button turns the corpus into reusable assets — it mines recurring tool-call workflows and failure→fix patterns across sessions and (when AI is configured) proposes concrete **skills**, **AGENTS.md rules**, and tooling gaps, each downloadable as a drop-in `SKILL.md` / `AGENTS.md` file. * **Analysis Eval**: A **🧪 Eval** button scores the quality of the AI analyses with six deterministic checks (schema completeness, actionability, conciseness, non-degeneracy, error coverage), plus an opt-in **LLM-as-judge panel** (strict/balanced/pragmatic lenses, averaged, with the panel's spread shown). Runs are **savable** into a history you can delete, **A/B compare**, chart (score + per-check sparklines), and export to **CSV**. * **Prompt Lab**: A **🔬 Prompt Lab** button closes the loop — edit two analysis prompt variants, and it re-analyzes a small sample of sessions with each and scores them with the deterministic eval (the eval is the fitness function), showing which prompt wins. * **Search & Filtering**: Includes a text search input to find sessions, and a dropdown to filter sessions by source/agent type (Antigravity CLI, IDE, Agent, OpenAI Codex, or Claude Code). * **Sorting & Refreshing**: Provides toggle controls to sort sessions chronologically and a refresh button to load new sessions. * **Session Metadata**: Hovering over a session displays an overview popover containing metadata such as step counts and session IDs. * **Adjustable Layout**: The sidebar features a drag handle to resize its width or collapse it entirely. **Timeline & Navigation** * **Proportional Timeline**: Displays a visual bar representing the elapsed wall-clock duration of the session, mapping active sequences and idle gaps proportionally. * **Viewport Tracking**: A translucent indicator moves across the timeline to highlight the exact time span of the transcript steps currently visible on the screen. * **Interactive Scrubbing**: Clicking the timeline auto-scrolls the transcript to the corresponding chronological point. * **Duration Metrics**: Hovering over timeline segments displays start/end timestamps and elapsed durations. **Transcript Rendering** * **Sequence Grouping**: Raw JSONL steps are grouped into collapsible sequences triggered by user inputs, displaying the calculated wall-clock duration of each sequence. * **Step Formatting**: Steps are formatted as individual UI cards depending on their actor (User, Model, Tool, System), with syntax highlighting for code and tool outputs. **Content Filtering & Search** * **Step Filtering**: Toggles to show or hide specific step types (User Queries, Tool Calls, Errors, Model Responses). Empty sequence containers are automatically hidden when filters are applied. * **In-Transcript Search**: A find-in-page text search utility to navigate through text matches within the active transcript. **AI Summarization** * **Backend Integration**: The Micronaut backend integrates with an LLM via LangChain4j — either the hosted **Google Gemini** API or a **local Ollama** model (see [Running the Application](#running-the-application-from-sources)). * **Session Summaries**: Analyzes raw JSONL transcripts via LLM to generate a high-level overview of the agent's actions and outcomes. * **Summary Panel**: The generated summary is injected into a collapsible panel at the top of the transcript view. ## Technology Stack & Implementation This project prioritizes a lightweight, high-performance, and maintainable architecture: - **Backend**: Built with [Micronaut](https://micronaut.io/) (Java). It serves the frontend static assets, receives pushed trajectories at its ingest API (normalizing each tool's format into a shared schema), and serves the session list, transcripts, and analyses from a Postgres store via plain JDBC. - **AI Integration**: Powered by [LangChain4j](https://github.com/langchain4j/langchain4j) connecting directly to [Google Gemini models](https://docs.langchain4j.dev/integrations/language-models/google-genai/). It uses chunking and recursive consolidation to process large transcript files that exceed standard token limits. - **Frontend**: A zero-build Vanilla JavaScript, HTML, and CSS single-page application. It avoids heavy framework overhead, relying instead on standard browser DOM APIs, customized CSS grid/flexbox layouts, and minimal dependencies (`marked.js` and `highlight.js`) for efficient rendering and responsiveness. ---

toolUseResult.stdout:
# Agent Brain Visualizer

## What is this project? The Agent Brain Visualizer is a dedicated companion tool for developers working with AI coding agents. Agents construct complex reasoning chains, dispatch background tasks, spawn subagents, and execute system commands over long-running sessions, recording all of these interactions in detailed JSONL transcript files (the agent's "brain"). It reads sessions from Antigravity (CLI/IDE/Agent), OpenAI Codex, and Claude Code. This visualizer parses those raw JSONL brain transcripts and renders them in a scannable and interactive web interface, allowing developers to inspect the agent's exact decision-making process. ## How it works The visualizer reads agent sessions from a shared [Postgres store](#the-trajectory-store--postgres). Trajectories get there by being **pushed**: the [`agent-ingest`](cli/README.md) CLI scans a machine's local transcripts (Antigravity, OpenAI Codex, Claude Code) and uploads them to the app, which normalizes and stores them. So a session captured on one computer is visible from any machine pointed at the same store. When you select a session from the sidebar, the application renders its normalized steps into interactive sequences. Additionally, it leverages Google's Gemini LLMs to automatically generate comprehensive executive summaries of long conversations—distilling thousands of lines of transcript into the core user intent, key technical decisions, and the final outcome of the session. These summaries are cached in the store, so a summary generated on one machine is available on the others. ### Key Features **Session Management** * **Session Loading**: Lists the ingested sessions for the selected source from the store, newest first. * **Multiple Sources**: Antigravity (`antigravity-cli` / `antigravity-ide`), **OpenAI Codex**, and **Claude Code** sessions are all ingested into the same store and rendered in one timeline view. The `agent-ingest` CLI locates each tool's transcripts (`~/.gemini`, `~/.codex/sessions`, `~/.claude/projects`) and pushes them; the server normalizes each tool's format into the shared step schema and caches AI summaries alongside them. > **Note:** Inline file preview is available for Antigravity transcripts, which embed explicit file references. Codex and Claude Code sessions reference files through shell/tool calls rather than the structured `file://` links Antigravity emits, so clickable file previews are not generated for them. * **Fleet Insights**: A **📊 Insights** button opens a cross-session dashboard for the selected source — session/outcome counts, busiest tools, the most common errors, average tool calls and duration, and a rolled-up backlog of the recommendations and issues surfaced by the per-session AI analyses. Every tally row is **drill-down**: click it to see (and open) the sessions behind it. * **Skill / AGENTS.md Miner**: A **🛠️ Mine** button turns the corpus into reusable assets — it mines recurring tool-call workflows and failure→fix patterns across sessions and (when AI is configured) proposes concrete **skills**, **AGENTS.md rules**, and tooling gaps, each downloadable as a drop-in `SKILL.md` / `AGENTS.md` file. * **Analysis Eval**: A **🧪 Eval** button scores the quality of the AI analyses with six deterministic checks (schema completeness, actionability, conciseness, non-degeneracy, error coverage), plus an opt-in **LLM-as-judge panel** (strict/balanced/pragmatic lenses, averaged, with the panel's spread shown). Runs are **savable** into a history you can delete, **A/B compare**, chart (score + per-check sparklines), and export to **CSV**. * **Prompt Lab**: A **🔬 Prompt Lab** button closes the loop — edit two analysis prompt variants, and it re-analyzes a small sample of sessions with each and scores them with the deterministic eval (the eval is the fitness function), showing which prompt wins. * **Search & Filtering**: Includes a text search input to find sessions, and a dropdown to filter sessions by source/agent type (Antigravity CLI, IDE, Agent, OpenAI Codex, or Claude Code). * **Sorting & Refreshing**: Provides toggle controls to sort sessions chronologically and a refresh button to load new sessions. * **Session Metadata**: Hovering over a session displays an overview popover containing metadata such as step counts and session IDs. * **Adjustable Layout**: The sidebar features a drag handle to resize its width or collapse it entirely. **Timeline & Navigation** * **Proportional Timeline**: Displays a visual bar representing the elapsed wall-clock duration of the session, mapping active sequences and idle gaps proportionally. * **Viewport Tracking**: A translucent indicator moves across the timeline to highlight the exact time span of the transcript steps currently visible on the screen. * **Interactive Scrubbing**: Clicking the timeline auto-scrolls the transcript to the corresponding chronological point. * **Duration Metrics**: Hovering over timeline segments displays start/end timestamps and elapsed durations. **Transcript Rendering** * **Sequence Grouping**: Raw JSONL steps are grouped into collapsible sequences triggered by user inputs, displaying the calculated wall-clock duration of each sequence. * **Step Formatting**: Steps are formatted as individual UI cards depending on their actor (User, Model, Tool, System), with syntax highlighting for code and tool outputs. **Content Filtering & Search** * **Step Filtering**: Toggles to show or hide specific step types (User Queries, Tool Calls, Errors, Model Responses). Empty sequence containers are automatically hidden when filters are applied. * **In-Transcript Search**: A find-in-page text search utility to navigate through text matches within the active transcript. **AI Summarization** * **Backend Integration**: The Micronaut backend integrates with an LLM via LangChain4j — either the hosted **Google Gemini** API or a **local Ollama** model (see [Running the Application](#running-the-application-from-sources)). * **Session Summaries**: Analyzes raw JSONL transcripts via LLM to generate a high-level overview of the agent's actions and outcomes. * **Summary Panel**: The generated summary is injected into a collapsible panel at the top of the transcript view. ## Technology Stack & Implementation This project prioritizes a lightweight, high-performance, and maintainable architecture: - **Backend**: Built with [Micronaut](https://micronaut.io/) (Java). It serves the frontend static assets, receives pushed trajectories at its ingest API (normalizing each tool's format into a shared schema), and serves the session list, transcripts, and analyses from a Postgres store via plain JDBC. - **AI Integration**: Powered by [LangChain4j](https://github.com/langchain4j/langchain4j) connecting directly to [Google Gemini models](https://docs.langchain4j.dev/integrations/language-models/google-genai/). It uses chunking and recursive consolidation to process large transcript files that exceed standard token limits. - **Frontend**: A zero-build Vanilla JavaScript, HTML, and CSS single-page application. You are reviewing the Java/Micronaut backend of the Agent Brain Visualizer repo at /home/scottdensmore/Developer/scottdensmore/agent-brain-visualizer (source under src/main/java, resources under src/main/resources; ignore build/). The app ingests AI-agent session transcripts via an HTTP API, normalizes them into a shared step schema, stores them in Postgres via plain JDBC, and serves session lists/transcripts/AI analyses (LangChain4j + Gemini or Ollama).

Find concrete, high-value improvement opportunities. Look for:
- Correctness bugs (edge cases, null handling, concurrency, resource leaks, SQL issues like injection/missing indexes/N+1, transaction handling)
- Security issues (unauthenticated endpoints, secrets handling, input validation on the ingest API, path traversal)
- Performance problems (loading whole transcripts into memory, missing pagination, repeated parsing)
- Architecture/maintainability issues (god classes, duplicated normalization logic, missing tests for tricky code)
- API design problems (error responses, status codes)

Return a prioritized list of findings. For each: severity (high/med/low), file:line, one-sentence problem statement, and a concrete suggested fix. Only include real findings you verified in the code — no speculation. Return raw findings, not prose for a human.

content:
<task-notification>
<task-id>a69bfbb8c5bd43f24</task-id>
<tool-use-id>REDACTED</tool-use-id>
<output-file>/tmp/claude-1000/-home-scottdensmore-Developer-scottdensmore-agent-brain-visualizer/c21c3560-2710-4c07-927e-0fd1f08468da/tasks/a69bfbb8c5bd43f24.output</output-file>
<status>completed</status>
<summary>Agent "Review Go CLI" finished</summary>
<note>A task-notification fires each time this agent stops with no live background children of its own. The user can send it another message and resume it, so the same task-id may notify more than once.</note>
<result>Findings verified against the code. Prioritized list:

## HIGH

**1. Manifest responses truncated at 1 MiB → large corpora fail every run**
`internal/client/client.go:157` — `io.ReadAll(io.LimitReader(resp.Body, 1<<20))` caps *all* response bodies (including successful GET manifest / summary-manifest) at 1 MiB; a manifest is an `id→hash` map (~110 bytes/entry), so past ~9k stored sessions the JSON is silently truncated and `json.Unmarshal` fails, making the source unsyncable. Fix: apply the 1 MiB `LimitReader` only to error bodies; read success bodies unbounded (or with a much higher cap), keying the branch on `resp.StatusCode`.

**2. No retry on transient failures; one blip fails the whole source**
`main.go:165,195,211` and `client.go:145` — a single network hiccup or 5xx on the manifest fetch or any push batch aborts that source (`continue`/`break`) with no retry/backoff, defeating the "safe to run on a schedule" goal. Fix: wrap `do` (or the idempotent GET/POST calls) in bounded retry-with-backoff on connection errors and 5xx/429.

## MED

**3. Every file is fully read and SHA-256'd on every run, even when unchanged**
`internal/scan/scan.go:198,208,214` — the diff is hash-based against the server manifest, but there is no local incremental state, so `os.ReadFile` + `ToValidUTF8` + `Sha256Hex` runs over the entire corpus each invocation regardless of the `Mtime` already captured. Fix: persist a local `id → {mtime,size,hash}` cache and skip re-hashing files whose mtime+size are unchanged.

**4. Whole transcripts and whole batches held in memory**
`scan.go:198` (`os.ReadFile` of each transcript) plus `client.go:92`/`:126` (`json.Marshal` of the full batch) mean a batch holds every transcript's bytes twice (raw string + marshaled JSON) in memory; combined with `maxBatchBytes = 16 MiB` of raw this is bounded per-batch but each individual huge file is still fully resident and JSON-copied. Fix: stream-encode batches (e.g. `json.Encoder` over an `io.Pipe` into the request body) instead of `json.Marshal` into a `[]byte`.

**5. Bearer token can be sent over cleartext http to a remote host**
`main.go:333` (`validateServer` accepts any `http://` host) + `client.go:146` — a non-localhost `--server http://...` with `AGENT_INGEST_TOKEN` set transmits the bearer token in plaintext with no warning. Fix: error or warn when a token is configured and the server scheme is `http` and host is not loopback.

**6. No signal handling; Ctrl-C cannot cancel in-flight work**
`main.go:71` uses `context.Background()` — SIGINT/SIGTERM won't cancel the HTTP requests (only the per-request 60 s timeout applies), so an interrupted run may leave a partially pushed source. Fix: derive ctx from `signal.NotifyContext(context.Background(), os.Interrupt, syscall.SIGTERM)`.

**7. Same filename stem in different project dirs collides on ID**
`scan.go:120` (Claude/Codex id = filename stem, "never from the path") — two genuinely different sessions sharing a stem across project directories both scan with the same `ID`, so `changedSessions`/manifest key collide and both get pushed under one id (last-writer-wins on the server). Fix: detect duplicate `(source,id)` during scan and warn/skip, or qualify the id with a path hash when a collision occurs.

## LOW

**8. Batch ordering is nondeterministic**
`main.go:250` `groupBySource` preserves filesystem `WalkDir` order and `SortedByKey` (`scan.go:243`) is defined but never called anywhere — batches (and thus retries/partial-failure boundaries) vary run to run. Fix: sort each source's sessions (use the existing `SortedByKey`) before `chunk`.

**9. `report.write` JSON/stdout write errors swallowed**
`main.go:464` `_ = enc.Encode(payload)` — a failed stdout write (e.g. broken pipe) is ignored and still returns exit 0. Fix: on encode/write error, report to stderr and return `exitFailed`.

**10. Redundant empty-file checks with a stat/read TOCTOU**
`scan.go:195` and `:202` both guard size 0 (stat then re-check after read); the stat-then-read window also means size can change between calls. Minor; could drop the stat and rely solely on `len(raw)==0`.

## Missing test coverage

- **Large-manifest truncation (finding 1):** no test drives a manifest body >1 MiB through `client.do`; would have caught the `LimitReader` cap.
- **`--batch-size < 1` usage error:** `main.go:120-123` validation is untested (no test asserts exit 2 for `--batch-size 0`).
- **Mid-batch push failure:** no test verifies that when batch 2 of 3 fails (`main.go:199` break), batch-1's `Ingested` count is retained, later batches are skipped, and the source is marked errored.
- **Duplicate stem collision (finding 7):** no scan test for two files with the same stem in different dirs.
- **Cleartext-token warning (finding 5):** no test around token + `http://` remote.
- **`serverMessage` fallbacks:** `client.go:176` non-JSON body and empty-body (status-line) branches are untested (existing tests only cover the `{"error":...}` path).</result>

**2. No retry on transient failures; one blip fails the whole source**
`main.go:165,195,211` and `client.go:145` — a single network hiccup or 5xx on the manifest fetch or any push batch aborts that source (`continue`/`break`) with no retry/backoff, defeating the "safe to run on a schedule" goal. The backend, frontend, and build/ops reviews are still running; I'll compile everything into one prioritized list once they finish.

content:
<task-notification>
<task-id>a045ea5e5b82e64e4</task-id>
<tool-use-id>REDACTED</tool-use-id>
<output-file>/tmp/claude-1000/-home-scottdensmore-Developer-scottdensmore-agent-brain-visualizer/c21c3560-2710-4c07-927e-0fd1f08468da/tasks/a045ea5e5b82e64e4.output</output-file>
<status>completed</status>
<summary>Agent "Review JS frontend" finished</summary>
<note>A task-notification fires each time this agent stops with no live background children of its own. The user can send it another message and resume it, so the same task-id may notify more than once.</note>
<result>Read all frontend files fully. Here are the verified findings, prioritized.

## HIGH severity

**1. Stored XSS via `marked.parse()` on untrusted transcript content**
`modules/timeline.js:158, 208, 215, 223, 232, 237, 340`
`marked.parse()` is called on `step.thinking` and `step.content` (raw agent/user transcript text) and the result is injected via `body.innerHTML` (line 443). marked.js does not sanitize by default and there is no DOMPurify anywhere in the codebase — a transcript containing `<img src=x onerror=...>` or `<script>`-equivalent markdown/HTML executes in the app origin. This is the single highest-value issue: transcript content is explicitly untrusted, and it reaches the DOM unescaped on every session view.
Fix: run marked output through DOMPurify (`DOMPurify.sanitize(marked.parse(x))`), added as a dependency, before assigning to `innerHTML`. Configure marked with a hook or wrap all six call sites in one helper.

**2. XSS via unescaped tool name**
`modules/timeline.js:434` — `<div class="tool-name">⚙ ${tool.name}</div>` interpolates `tool.name` (from transcript `tool_calls`) into `innerHTML` with no `escapeHtml`.
`modules/stats.js:266` — same `tool.name`/`toolName` used unescaped in both `title="${name}"` and the text node (note the sibling error chart at line 293 *does* call `escapeHtml`, so this is an inconsistency, not intent).
Fix: wrap both in `escapeHtml(tool.name)` / `escapeHtml(name)`.

**3. XSS via unescaped `step.type` / `step.source`**
`modules/timeline.js:113-124` — `typeStr`/`sourceStr` come straight from `step.type`/`step.source` and are injected into `header.innerHTML` (badge + label) with no escaping. If ingested data has non-enum type/source strings, HTML executes.
Fix: `escapeHtml(typeStr)` and `escapeHtml(sourceStr)`.

**4. Race condition on transcript load (no generation guard)**
`app.js:520-576` `selectConversation` fetches the transcript with no supersede guard. Clicking session A then B quickly: if A's fetch resolves after B's, `renderTranscript`/`renderStats` run with A's data into the now-B container, showing the wrong transcript. `modules/eval.js:478-507` already implements the correct `renderGeneration` pattern — `selectConversation` and `triggerAnalysis`'s transcript path lack it (analysis has `currentPollSessionId`, transcript has nothing).
Fix: adopt the same monotonic-generation guard before writing to `#transcript-container`.

## MEDIUM severity

**5. Attribute-injection XSS via web-search citation URLs**
`modules/timeline.js:294-323` — `linkMap` URLs are matched by `https?://[^\s\)]+`, which permits a `"` character, then injected into `href="${linkMap[id]}"`. A search-result URL like `https://x"onmouseover=alert(1)` breaks out of the attribute. Search results are untrusted content.
Fix: `escapeHtml` the URL (or build the anchor via DOM APIs / restrict the char class to exclude quotes).

**6. Infinite progress-poll loop on analysis fetch failure**
`modules/analysis.js:68-97` — `polling` is set `false` only after `await fetch(url)` resolves (line 97). If that fetch throws, control jumps to `catch` (line 211) and `polling` is never cleared, so the `pollProgress` while-loop keeps hitting `/progress` every 1s indefinitely while the user stays on that session.
Fix: set `polling = false` in a `finally`, or gate the loop on a flag cleared in `finally`.

**7. No virtualization; full transcript rendered eagerly**
`modules/timeline.js:33-446` — every step becomes a DOM subtree up front (map over all steps), each running `marked.parse` and `JSON.parse`, and `ui.js:230-253` registers a MutationObserver + two IntersectionObservers per card. For sessions with thousands of steps this is heavy layout/parse work on the main thread with no windowing.
Fix: virtualize (render on scroll / IntersectionObserver-driven lazy body parse), or at least defer `marked.parse` of a step body until its card is first expanded (bodies start collapsed anyway).

**8. Keyboard inaccessibility of primary controls**
`app.js:392-399` conversation items are `<div class="conv-item">` with a click handler but no `role`, `tabindex`, or keydown handler — sessions cannot be selected by keyboard. Same pattern for the stat cards (`modules/stats.js:364-453`) and drill rows (`modules/drilldown.js`). Selection is mouse-only.
Fix: use `<button>` or add `role="button"`, `tabindex="0"`, and Enter/Space handlers.

**9. Modal has no focus management / focus trap**
`index.html:127-137`, `modules/ui.js:95-126` — opening the file-preview modal does not move focus into it or trap Tab; background remains focusable. Escape-to-close exists (good) but focus is never restored to the trigger.
Fix: focus the close button on open, trap Tab within the modal, restore focus on close.

## LOW severity

**10. CDN scripts loaded without SRI or version pinning**
`index.html:123` loads `marked/marked.min.js` (no version, no `integrity`); `:124` highlight.js and `:8,11` CSS/fonts also lack SRI. Unpinned marked can silently change escaping behavior; no SRI is a supply-chain exposure.
Fix: pin exact versions and add `integrity`/`crossorigin`.

**11. Duplicated fetch/error-handling and constants across modules**
`FLAVOR_LABELS` is copy-pasted in `insights.js:19`, `mine.js:28`, `eval.js:20`, `optimize.js:18`; `section()` and `round1()` are re-defined in several modules; the `container.innerHTML = 'loading…' / try fetch / catch red-error` block is repeated in `insights.js`, `mine.js`, `eval.js`, `optimize.js`, `app.js`. 
Fix: extract a shared `flavorLabels`, `section`, `round1`, and a `fetchJson(url)` + `renderError(container)` helper.

**12. Global mutable state via `window.*` and `state`**
`modules/stats.js:166-167` writes `window.timelineStart`/`window.timelineTotalMs`, read in `timeline.js:568` and `ui.js:147`; `window.scrollToTime` (timeline.js:577) and inline `onclick` handlers in `stats.js:216,238` depend on these globals. This couples stats/timeline/ui through the global namespace and makes render ordering load-bearing (stats must run to populate the timeline marker).
Fix: move shared timeline bounds into the imported `state` object.

**13. `summaryCache` keyed by session id only, not flavor**
`modules/utils.js:24`, `modules/analysis.js:38-41` — cache key is `sessionId`; if ids collide across flavors the wrong cached analysis renders. Minor given ids are likely globally unique, but the flavor is available and free to include.

**14. `renderTranscript` regex meta-parsing runs on every render with no guard for very large `content`**
`modules/timeline.js:198-340` runs multiple global regexes (tag extraction, citation grouping) over full `step.content` strings; a pathological large content field does redundant passes. Low impact but worth batching the passes.

No issues found in `mine-export.js`/`eval-export.js` (CSV cell quoting at `eval-export.js:31-33` and YAML quoting at `mine-export.js:25-29` are correctly escaped); insights/mine/eval/optimize render paths consistently use `escapeHtml` for model/transcript-derived strings — the escaping gaps are concentrated in `timeline.js` and `stats.js`.</result>

content.text: The frontend review is done too, and it found the most serious issue so far: stored XSS — transcript content (which is untrusted agent/user output) is rendered through `marked.parse()` into `innerHTML` with no sanitizer, plus a few unescaped interpolation spots in `timeline.js` and `stats.js`. <task-notification>
<task-id>a424a3f3888bc0c36</task-id>
<tool-use-id>REDACTED</tool-use-id>
<output-file>/tmp/claude-1000/-home-scottdensmore-Developer-scottdensmore-agent-brain-visualizer/c21c3560-2710-4c07-927e-0fd1f08468da/tasks/a424a3f3888bc0c36.output</output-file>

<summary>Agent "Review build/tests/ops" finished</summary>
<note>A task-notification fires each time this agent stops with no live background children of its own. The user can send it another message and resume it, so the same task-id may notify more than once.</note>
<result>Findings verified against the repo. Raw prioritized list below.

## HIGH

1. **HIGH — `.github/workflows/ci.yml` (no docker job)** — The `Dockerfile` is never built or run by any workflow (`ci.yml`, `release-cli.yml`, `release-native-binaries.yml` contain no `docker build`), so a broken image (bad `cp` glob, base-tag drift, non-root perms) ships undetected. Fix: add a CI job that runs `docker compose --profile full up -d --build` (or `docker build .`) and curls `/health`.

2. **HIGH — `docker-compose.yml:23-24,56-57` + `docker-compose.prod.yml:15-26`** — The Postgres password is a hardcoded literal `agentviz` in the base compose and the prod override only resets `ports`, so a "production" deploy inherits the well-known default DB credentials. Fix: source `POSTGRES_PASSWORD`/`POSTGRES_USER` from env (`${...}`) and require them (no default) in `docker-compose.prod.yml`.

## MED

3. **MED — `build.gradle:128-132`** — Spotless JS formatting targets only `src/main/resources/public/**`; the test/e2e JavaScript (`e2e/*.js`, `e2e/*.mjs`, `src/test/js/*.js`) is neither formatted nor linted, and there is no ESLint anywhere (no `.eslintrc`, no `lint` script in `package.json`). Fix: extend the Spotless `javascript` target to `e2e/**` and `src/test/js/**`, and/or add ESLint with a CI step.

4. **MED — `src/main/java/.../{AnalyzerService,AnalysisJudgeService,VariantAnalyzerService,MinerAdvisorService}.java`** — The four LLM-backed services have no matching `*Test` (only their controllers do); their prompt-assembly/response-mapping logic is untested. Fix: add unit tests with a stubbed `ChatModel` asserting request construction and response parsing.

5. **MED — `README.md:408`** — Claims "Three suites cover the app; CI runs all of them," but `ci.yml` actually runs five jobs (backend, frontend-unit, e2e, **Go CLI**, **native smoke**); the Go CLI suite is absent from the Development & Tests section entirely. Fix: update the count and add the `cd cli && go test ./...` step.

6. **MED — `package.json:13-15`** — Dev deps use floating caret ranges and are behind (`vitest ^2.1.8` → v3 available, `jsdom ^25` → v26, `@playwright/test ^1.49.1`); lockfile pins them but there is no renovate/dependabot and no Gradle version catalog (versions inline in `build.gradle`). Fix: add Dependabot for npm+gradle+actions and bump.

## LOW

7. **LOW — `Dockerfile:39`** — No `HEALTHCHECK` instruction; liveness is only defined in `docker-compose.yml`, so an image run outside compose (k8s, plain `docker run`) has no health signal. Fix: add a `HEALTHCHECK` mirroring the compose `/dev/tcp` probe.

8. **LOW — `README.md:433`** — LICENSE link is `[LICENSE](../LICENSE)`, but the file is `./LICENSE` at repo root alongside this README, so the link points outside the repo. Fix: change to `(LICENSE)`.

9. **LOW — `mise.toml:1-4`** — Pins only `java = "temurin-25"`; Node and Go are unpinned, so local contributors can drift from CI's Node 22 / Go 1.23. Fix: add `node = "22"` and `go = "1.23"`.

10. **LOW — `cli/go.mod:1`** — Module path `github.com/scottdensmore/antigravity-brain-visualizer/cli` is inconsistent with the Java group `io.github.glaforge.agybrainviz` and the repo directory `agent-brain-visualizer`. Fix: reconcile the module path with the canonical repo URL.

11. **LOW — `e2e/eval.spec.js:28-45`** — The "saving a run" specs persist rows into the shared Postgres store (`#save-run-btn`) that later A/B-comparison assertions read; with `retries: 1` (`playwright.config.js:28`) a mid-spec failure re-runs a mutating flow against a non-reset DB, risking duplicate rows / order coupling. Fix: scope saved-run assertions to run-specific identifiers or clean up in an `afterEach`.

12. **LOW — `.github/workflows/ci.yml:63` vs `release-cli.yml:19`** — The CLI Go version is hardcoded `"1.23"` in `ci.yml` but read via `go-version-file: cli/go.mod` in the release workflow, so a future Go bump silently desyncs the two pipelines. Fix: use `go-version-file: cli/go.mod` in `ci.yml` too.

Non-findings confirmed (so they aren't re-flagged): `.env` is git-ignored and NOT tracked (only `.env.example`); Dockerfile runtime runs as non-root uid 10001; compose and native jobs have healthchecks; Go CLI is fully built/vetted/gofmt-checked/tested in CI; no hard waits (`waitForTimeout`/`sleep`) in any e2e spec; all frontend modules have matching vitest files.</result>

<task-notification>
<task-id>a9cbec04f5b44edd6</task-id>
<tool-use-id>REDACTED</tool-use-id>
<output-file>/tmp/claude-1000/-home-scottdensmore-Developer-scottdensmore-agent-brain-visualizer/c21c3560-2710-4c07-927e-0fd1f08468da/tasks/a9cbec04f5b44edd6.output</output-file>
<status>completed</status>
<summary>Agent "Review Java backend" finished</summary>
<note>A task-notification fires each time this agent stops with no live background children of its own. The user can send it another message and resume it, so the same task-id may notify more than once.</note>
<result>Based on my review of the Micronaut backend, here are the prioritized findings. All verified against the code.

## HIGH

**1. Expensive LLM + DB-mutating endpoints are entirely unauthenticated**
`IngestAuthFilter.java:40` — `@ServerFilter("/api/ingest/**")` only guards ingest. Every other controller is open: `AnalysisController` `/summarize` (spends LLM tokens, and `force=true` deletes the cache and recomputes — `AnalysisController.java:119`), plus `/api/optimize` (POST), `/api/mine`, `/api/insights`, and `/api/eval` (POST/DELETE at `EvalController.java:54,68`, which write/delete DB rows). The design explicitly contemplates the server being reachable off-localhost (that's the whole point of `INGEST_TOKEN`), so an unauthenticated attacker can burn the Gemini API budget, DoS via chunked analysis, and mutate `eval_runs`.
Fix: broaden the auth filter (or add a second `@ServerFilter`) to also cover the token-spending / mutating endpoints, or introduce a shared read/compute token distinct from the ingest token.

## MED

**2. `summarize` is a state-mutating, expensive operation exposed as GET**
`AnalysisController.java:89` — `@Get(".../summarize")` deletes the cached summary on `force=true` (line 119), runs the LLM pipeline, and upserts the result (line 283). GETs must be safe/idempotent; a browser prefetch, proxy, or crawler hitting `?force=true` will silently trigger token spend and cache invalidation.
Fix: make it `@Post`.

**3. `AnalysisController` is a god class doing orchestration in the controller, with `System.out`/`printStackTrace` logging**
`AnalysisController.java` (404 lines) embeds chunking, parallel dispatch, progress simulation, recursive consolidation, and local merge directly in the HTTP handler. It logs via `System.out.println` (line 148), `System.err.println` + `e.printStackTrace()` (lines 176–179, 257, 285, 291–292) instead of SLF4J like the rest of the codebase.
Fix: extract an `AnalysisOrchestrator` service; replace stdout/stderr with a `Logger`.

**4. `progressMap` / `runningTasks` keyed by `id` only, ignoring `flavor`**
`AnalysisController.java:45,86` (used at 101, 134, 194, 301, 304) — two sessions from different sources that share an id collide in the in-flight guard and progress map. It's also purely in-memory, so nothing coordinates across instances that share the DB.
Fix: key both maps by `(flavor, id)`.

**5. `new ObjectMapper()` allocated per request / per call**
`AnalysisController.java:97` and `:122` construct a fresh `ObjectMapper` on every request; `TranscriptParser.java:46` constructs one on every `parseSequences` call. `ObjectMapper` is thread-safe and expensive to build (`AnalysisSequences`/`Ingestor`/`AntigravityNormalizer` correctly use a static one).
Fix: reuse a single `static final ObjectMapper`.

**6. File-preview endpoint does not resolve symlinks**
`BrainController.java:92-97` — `getFileContent` normalizes the path and checks `startsWith(geminiRoot)`, which blocks `..` traversal (well covered by tests) but does not call `toRealPath()`. A symlink placed under `~/.gemini` pointing outside is followed by `Files.readString`, escaping the sandbox. This endpoint is also unauthenticated.
Fix: resolve `filePath.toRealPath()` and re-check `startsWith` against `geminiRoot().toRealPath()`.

## LOW

**7. Whole transcript loaded into memory on both ingest and read**
`Ingestor.java:145` (`raw.lines().toList()`) plus `AntigravityNormalizer.toStepsJson` building a full in-memory `ArrayNode`, and `BrainController.java:76` / `SessionRepository.steps` returning the entire steps JSON as one `String`. With `max-request-size: 128 MiB` (`application.yml:11`) a single large session pins substantial heap per in-flight request.
Fix: cap per-item raw size at ingest; consider streaming the transcript read.

**8. Partial ingest failures return HTTP 200 with no signal in the status**
`IngestController.java:69,89` — `ingest`/`ingestSummaries` return `IngestResult` (with a `failed` count) always as 200; a caller that only checks status can't tell that items were rejected.
Fix: document that clients must inspect `failed`, or return a 207/4xx when `failed > 0`.

**9. `fakeProgress` sleeper occupies an IO-pool thread**
`AnalysisController.java:231-248` submits a `Thread.sleep(2000)` loop (up to ~18s) onto the shared `TaskExecutors.IO` pool during consolidation, consuming a pool thread purely to animate the progress bar.
Fix: use a scheduled executor / lighter timer, or drive progress from the consolidation callbacks.

**10. Default model names look invalid**
`AiConfig.java:50,52` — defaults `gemini-3.5-flash` and `gemma4` are not standard model identifiers; if an operator relies on the defaults, the first LLM call fails at runtime rather than at startup.
Fix: verify/correct the default model names, or validate model availability on startup.

Note: SQL is uniformly parameterized (no injection), the `(source, updated_at DESC, id)` index backs the list query, pagination is present and clamped (`BrainController.java:46-48,63`), JDBC resources use try-with-resources throughout, and the bearer-token comparison is constant-time (`IngestAuthFilter.java:84-89`) — no findings there.</result>
<usage><subagent_tokens>71153</subagent_tokens><tool_uses>30</tool_uses><duration_ms>163474</duration_ms></usage>
</task-notification>

content.text: I reviewed the whole app with four parallel deep-dives (Java backend, JS frontend, Go CLI, build/test/deploy) and spot-verified the top claims myself. The codebase is in good shape overall — parameterized SQL throughout, constant-time token comparison, non-root Docker image, no hard waits in e2e tests, every frontend module has a vitest file. But there are real issues worth fixing, and a few of them are serious. ## Fix first (security) 1. **Stored XSS in the transcript view.** Transcript content is untrusted (it's whatever the agent or user produced, pushed via the ingest API), yet `timeline.js` renders it with `marked.parse()` straight into `innerHTML` at seven call sites (`modules/timeline.js:158, 208–237, 340` → assigned at `:443`), and there's no DOMPurify anywhere in the codebase. A transcript containing `` executes in the app. There are also plain unescaped interpolations: `tool.name` at `timeline.js:434` and `stats.js:266`, `step.type`/`step.source` at `timeline.js:113-124`, and citation URLs injected into `href="…"` without quoting (`timeline.js:294-323`). Fix: one sanitizing helper (DOMPurify around marked output) used everywhere, plus `escapeHtml` on the raw interpolations. 2. **Almost every expensive/mutating endpoint is unauthenticated.** `IngestAuthFilter` (`IngestAuthFilter.java:40`) only covers `/api/ingest/**`. `/summarize` (spends Gemini tokens; `force=true` deletes the cache), `/api/optimize`, `/api/mine`, and `/api/eval` POST/DELETE (which write and delete DB rows) are all open — and the app is designed to be reachable off-localhost, that's why the ingest token exists. Broaden the filter or add a second token for compute/mutating routes. Related: `summarize` is a state-mutating, token-spending **GET** (`AnalysisController.java:89`) — should be POST. 3. **Prod deploy ships the default DB password.** `docker-compose.yml:23-24` hardcodes `agentviz`/`agentviz` and `docker-compose.prod.yml` only overrides ports, so production inherits well-known credentials. Require `${POSTGRES_PASSWORD}` from the environment with no default in prod. 4. **File-preview symlink escape.** `BrainController.java:92-97` blocks `..` traversal but never calls `toRealPath()`, so a symlink under `~/.gemini` pointing elsewhere is followed and read — and this endpoint is also unauthenticated. ## Correctness bugs - **CLI breaks on large corpora**: `cli/internal/client/client.go:157` caps *all* response bodies at 1 MiB, including the successful manifest GET. Past roughly 9k stored sessions the manifest JSON is truncated and the source becomes permanently unsyncable. The cap should apply only to error bodies. - **No retry in the CLI**: one transient 5xx or network blip aborts the whole source (`cli/main.go:165-211`) — bad for a tool meant to run on a schedule. Add bounded retry with backoff. - **Wrong-transcript race in the UI**: `app.js:520-576` has no supersede guard, so clicking session A then B quickly can render A's data into B's view. `eval.js` already implements the right generation-counter pattern — reuse it. - **Infinite polling loop**: if the analysis fetch throws, `polling` is never cleared (`modules/analysis.js:68-97`) and the UI hits `/progress` every second forever. Clear it in a `finally`. - **Analysis in-flight guard collides across sources**: `progressMap`/`runningTasks` are keyed by session id only, not `(flavor, id)` (`AnalysisController.java:45,86`). ## Performance - The transcript renders every step eagerly — thousands of `marked.parse` calls and observer registrations up front (`timeline.js:33-446`). Since card bodies start collapsed anyway, deferring markdown parsing until first expand would be a cheap big win; virtualization is the fuller fix. - The CLI re-reads and re-SHA-256s the entire local corpus on every run despite having mtimes in hand (`cli/internal/scan/scan.go:198-214`). A small local `id → {mtime,size,hash}` cache would make scheduled runs near-instant. - `new ObjectMapper()` per request in `AnalysisController.java:97,122` and per call in `TranscriptParser.java:46` — the rest of the codebase correctly uses a shared static one. ## Structural / process improvements - **CI never builds the Docker image**, so a broken Dockerfile ships undetected. Add a job that builds and curls `/health`. Also, `ci.yml` hardcodes Go `1.23` while the release workflow reads it from `go.mod` — they'll silently desync. - **`AnalysisController` is a 404-line god class** doing chunking, parallel dispatch, and consolidation inside the HTTP handler, logging via `System.out`/`printStackTrace` while everything else uses SLF4J. Extract an orchestrator service. - **Test gaps**: the four LLM-backed services (`AnalyzerService`, `AnalysisJudgeService`, `VariantAnalyzerService`, `MinerAdvisorService`) have no unit tests, and the CLI has no tests for the manifest-truncation path, mid-batch failure behavior, or duplicate-filename-stem collisions (which currently make two different sessions overwrite each other under one id — worth fixing too). - **Frontend duplication**: `FLAVOR_LABELS`, `section()`, `round1()`, and the load/error boilerplate are copy-pasted across `insights.js`, `mine.js`, `eval.js`, and `optimize.js`; timeline bounds travel via `window.*` globals. A small shared utils module would tighten this up. - Smaller items: no SRI/version pinning on the CDN scripts in `index.html`; no `HEALTHCHECK` in the Dockerfile; keyboard inaccessibility of session list/stat cards (mouse-only, no `tabindex`/key handlers); modal lacks focus trapping; `mise.toml` pins Java but not Node/Go; README's LICENSE link points to `../LICENSE`; default model names in `AiConfig.java:50,52` (`gemini-3.5-flash`, `gemma4`) don't look like valid identifiers. If you want to act on this, I'd sequence it as: (1) XSS sanitization + auth filter broadening + prod compose credentials as one security pass, (2) the CLI manifest cap + retry, (3) the UI race/polling bugs, then the performance and refactoring items. Want me to start on the security pass?

content.text: I'll work through the phases now. The CLI fixes are isolated in `cli/`, so I'll delegate those to an agent running in parallel while I do the security pass (frontend XSS + backend auth + compose credentials) myself.

input.prompt:
You are fixing verified issues in the Go ingest CLI at /home/scottdensmore/Developer/scottdensmore/agent-brain-visualizer/cli (main.go, internal/client/client.go, internal/scan/scan.go, plus tests). Work ONLY inside the cli/ directory. Do not commit; leave changes in the working tree.

Apply these fixes (verified findings from a code review):

1. HIGH — client.go:157: `io.ReadAll(io.LimitReader(resp.Body, 1<<20))` caps ALL response bodies at 1 MiB, silently truncating large successful manifest GETs (~9k+ sessions) and breaking sync. Apply the 1 MiB limit only to error responses (non-2xx); read success bodies without that cap.

2. HIGH — main.go + client.go: no retry on transient failures; one network blip or 5xx aborts the whole source. Add bounded retry with backoff (e.g. 3 attempts, exponential backoff with small jitter-free waits are fine) in the client for connection errors, 429, and 5xx responses. The GET manifest and push endpoints are idempotent so retrying is safe. Respect context cancellation during backoff sleeps.

3. MED — main.go:71: no signal handling. Derive the context from signal.NotifyContext(context.Background(), os.Interrupt, syscall.SIGTERM) so Ctrl-C cancels in-flight requests and backoff waits cleanly.

4. MED — main.go:333/client.go:146: a bearer token configured with a non-loopback http:// server sends the token in cleartext with no warning. Print a clear warning to stderr in that case (do not hard-error).

5. MED — scan.go: two different sessions with the same filename stem in different project dirs collide on ID (last-writer-wins on the server). Detect duplicate (source,id) during scan; on collision, keep the first and print a warning to stderr identifying both paths, or qualify the colliding id with a short path hash — pick whichever fits the existing code style better and is safe for existing stored ids (prefer warn+skip to avoid changing ids of already-ingested sessions).

6. LOW — main.go:250: batch ordering is nondeterministic; the existing scan.SortedByKey helper is never called. Sort each source's sessions before chunking into batches.

7. LOW — main.go:464: `_ = enc.Encode(payload)` swallows stdout write errors in report.write. On error, print to stderr and make the command exit with the failure exit code.

Also add tests for: manifest response body larger than 1 MiB round-tripping correctly; retry behavior (server returns 500 twice then 200 → succeeds; 400 → no retry); duplicate-stem collision handling in scan; and keep all existing tests passing.

Match the existing code style (error wrapping, flag handling, test table style). When done run: `cd /home/scottdensmore/Developer/scottdensmore/agent-brain-visualizer/cli && gofmt -l . && go vet ./... && go test ./...` and fix anything that fails.

Return: a summary of each change made (file:line), the final test output tail, and anything you deliberately did NOT do.

<result>All done. All checks pass: `gofmt -l` clean, `go vet` clean, and the full test run:

```
ok  	github.com/scottdensmore/antigravity-brain-visualizer/cli	5.338s
ok  	github.com/scottdensmore/antigravity-brain-visualizer/cli/internal/client	0.139s

```

## Changes made

**`/home/scottdensmore/Developer/scottdensmore/agent-brain-visualizer/cli/internal/client/client.go`**
- Fix 1 (client.go:182-215, `doOnce`): the 1 MiB `io.LimitReader` cap now applies only to non-2xx error bodies (which just feed the error message); success bodies are read in full with plain `io.ReadAll`, so a large manifest is never truncated.
- Fix 2 (client.go:33-51, 153-178): `Client` gained `attempts` (3) and `backoff` (250ms base, doubling) fields set in `New`. `do` now loops: connection errors, 429, and 5xx are retried; 4xx and JSON-decode errors are not; a cancelled context is never retried. POST bodies are rewound via `req.GetBody` before each retry, and the backoff sleep is a `select` on `req.Context().Done()` vs `time.After`, so Ctrl-C ends the wait immediately.

**`/home/scottdensmore/Developer/scottdensmore/agent-brain-visualizer/cli/main.go`**
- Fix 3 (main.go:63-69): `main` derives its context from `signal.NotifyContext(context.Background(), os.Interrupt, syscall.SIGTERM)`; `cli` now takes `ctx` as its first parameter and threads it into `run`.
- Fix 4 (main.go:162, 355-375): new `warnCleartextToken` prints a stderr warning when a token would be sent over `http://` to a host that is neither `localhost` nor a loopback IP; it never hard-errors.
- Fix 6 (main.go:167-168): each source's session list goes through `scan.SortedByKey` before diffing/chunking, so batch order is deterministic.
- Fix 7 (main.go:233-238, 502-560): `report.write` returns an error; a failed JSON encode is reported on stderr and the command exits with `exitFailed` (1).

**`/home/scottdensmore/Developer/scottdensmore/agent-brain-visualizer/cli/internal/scan/scan.go`**
- Fix 5 (scan.go:35-37, 113-143): `scanFlat` tracks a `seen` id→path map; a second file with the same stem is skipped (first wins, so already-stored ids never change) with a stderr warning naming the id and both paths. Warnings go through a package-level `warnWriter` (defaults to `os.Stderr`) so tests can capture them. Antigravity ids are directory names in a single dir, so only the flat walker needed this.

## Tests added
- `client_test.go`: `TestManifestLargerThanOneMiBRoundTripsUntruncated` (16k-entry manifest, ~1.3 MiB), `TestTransientServerErrorsAreRetried` (500, 500, 200 → succeeds in exactly 3 requests), `TestClientErrorsAreNotRetried` (400 → exactly 1 request), `TestRetriesGiveUpAfterTheAttemptBudget`, `TestPushRetryResendsTheFullBody` (verifies the body rewind on POST retry). Existing 503 test switched to a fast-backoff client.
- `scan_test.go`: `TestScanKeepsTheFirstOfDuplicateStemsAndWarns`, `TestScanDuplicateStemAfterAnEmptyFileIsNotACollision` (an empty first file doesn't shadow the real one).
- `main_test.go`: `TestCleartextTokenOverHTTPWarnsOnlyForNonLoopbackHosts` (table-driven); `runCLI` updated for the new `cli(ctx, ...)` signature. All 25 pre-existing tests still pass unchanged in behavior.

## Deliberately not done
- Did not qualify colliding ids with a path hash — chose warn+skip-first, as the task preferred, to avoid re-ingesting already-stored sessions under new ids.
- Did not add a retry flag/env knob or jitter; attempts and backoff are fixed constants in `client.New` (tests tune the unexported `backoff` field directly).
- Did not check errors on the plain-text `Fprintf` report lines — only the `--json` encode path fails the run, per the finding at main.go:464.
- Did not add an end-to-end CLI test for retry or batch ordering (client-level retry tests cover the behavior; ordering is a trivial `SortedByKey` call).
- Note: `git diff --stat` shows modified Java files under `src/main/java/...` — those changes are not mine (my work touched only `cli/`); they appear to come from a concurrent session, so I left them untouched.</result>

<note>A task-notification fires each time this agent stops with no live background children of its own. The user can send it another message and resume it, so the same task-id may notify more than once.</note>
<result>All done. All checks pass: `gofmt -l` clean, `go vet` clean, and the full test run:

```
ok  	github.com/scottdensmore/antigravity-brain-visualizer/cli	5.338s
ok  	github.com/scottdensmore/antigravity-brain-visualizer/cli/internal/client	0.139s
ok  	github.com/scottdensmore/antigravity-brain-visualizer/cli/internal/scan	0.013s
```

## Changes made

**`/home/scottdensmore/Developer/scottdensmore/agent-brain-visualizer/cli/internal/client/client.go`**
- Fix 1 (client.go:182-215, `doOnce`): the 1 MiB `io.LimitReader` cap now applies only to non-2xx error bodies (which just feed the error message); success bodies are read in full with plain `io.ReadAll`, so a large manifest is never truncated.
- Fix 2 (client.go:33-51, 153-178): `Client` gained `attempts` (3) and `backoff` (250ms base, doubling) fields set in `New`. `do` now loops: connection errors, 429, and 5xx are retried; 4xx and JSON-decode errors are not; a cancelled context is never retried. POST bodies are rewound via `req.GetBody` before each retry, and the backoff sleep is a `select` on `req.Context().Done()` vs `time.After`, so Ctrl-C ends the wait immediately.

**`/home/scottdensmore/Developer/scottdensmore/agent-brain-visualizer/cli/main.go`**
- Fix 3 (main.go:63-69): `main` derives its context from `signal.NotifyContext(context.Background(), os.Interrupt, syscall.SIGTERM)`; `cli` now takes `ctx` as its first parameter and threads it into `run`.
- Fix 4 (main.go:162, 355-375): new `warnCleartextToken` prints a stderr warning when a token would be sent over `http://` to a host that is neither `localhost` nor a loopback IP; it never hard-errors.
- Fix 6 (main.go:167-168): each source's session list goes through `scan.SortedByKey` before diffing/chunking, so batch order is deterministic.
- Fix 7 (main.go:233-238, 502-560): `report.write` returns an error; a failed JSON encode is reported on stderr and the command exits with `exitFailed` (1).

**`/home/scottdensmore/Developer/scottdensmore/agent-brain-visualizer/cli/internal/scan/scan.go`**
- Fix 5 (scan.go:35-37, 113-143): `scanFlat` tracks a `seen` id→path map; a second file with the same stem is skipped (first wins, so already-stored ids never change) with a stderr warning naming the id and both paths. Warnings go through a package-level `warnWriter` (defaults to `os.Stderr`) so tests can capture them. Antigravity ids are directory names in a single dir, so only the flat walker needed this.

## Tests added
- `client_test.go`: `TestManifestLargerThanOneMiBRoundTripsUntruncated` (16k-entry manifest, ~1.3 MiB), `TestTransientServerErrorsAreRetried` (500, 500, 200 → succeeds in exactly 3 requests), `TestClientErrorsAreNotRetried` (400 → exactly 1 request), `TestRetriesGiveUpAfterTheAttemptBudget`, `TestPushRetryResendsTheFullBody` (verifies the body rewind on POST retry). Existing 503 test switched to a fast-backoff client.
- `scan_test.go`: `TestScanKeepsTheFirstOfDuplicateStemsAndWarns`, `TestScanDuplicateStemAfterAnEmptyFileIsNotACollision` (an empty first file doesn't shadow the real one).
- `main_test.go`: `TestCleartextTokenOverHTTPWarnsOnlyForNonLoopbackHosts` (table-driven); `runCLI` updated for the new `cli(ctx, ...)` signature. All 25 pre-existing tests still pass unchanged in behavior.

## Deliberately not done
- Did not qualify colliding ids with a path hash — chose warn+skip-first, as the task preferred, to avoid re-ingesting already-stored sessions under new ids.
- Did not add a retry flag/env knob or jitter; attempts and backoff are fixed constants in `client.New` (tests tune the unexported `backoff` field directly).
- Did not check errors on the plain-text `Fprintf` report lines — only the `--json` encode path fails the run, per the finding at main.go:464.
- Did not add an end-to-end CLI test for retry or batch ordering (client-level retry tests cover the behavior; ordering is a trivial `SortedByKey` call).
- Note: `git diff --stat` shows modified Java files under `src/main/java/...` — those changes are not mine (my work touched only `cli/`); they appear to come from a concurrent session, so I left them untouched.</result>

file.content:
# Docker Compose for the Agent Brain Visualizer.
#
#   Database only (for local development / tests — the default):
#     docker compose up -d                       # start Postgres; data persists in the pgdata volume
#     docker compose up -d postgres              # same, explicit
#
#   Whole stack (app + database) in Docker:
#     docker compose --profile full up -d --build   # build the app image and run it against Postgres
#
#   Stop:
#     docker compose down        # stop, keeping the database volume
#     docker compose down -v     # stop and WIPE the database
#
# The credentials below match the defaults in `.env.example`, so `./gradlew run` (or the app
# container) works against this database with no extra configuration.
services:
  postgres:
    image: postgres:17-alpine
    container_name: agent-brain-viz-db
    restart: unless-stopped
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: agentbrainviz
      POSTGRES_USER: agentviz
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: agentviz
    ports:
      - "5432:5432"
    volumes:
      # Named volume: the data survives `docker compose down`, restarts, and app-image rebuilds.
      # It is removed only by `docker compose down -v`.
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U agentviz -d agentbrainviz"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 10

  app:
    build:
      context: .
      args:
        # Stamp the image version (shown at GET /health). Set to a release tag or `git describe`;
        # defaults to "docker" for an unlabelled local build.
        APP_VERSION: ${APP_VERSION:-docker}
    image: agent-brain-viz:local
    container_name: agent-brain-viz-app
    restart: unless-stopped
    # Only starts with `--profile full`, so the default `docker compose up` stays database-only for
    # local development (where you run the app with `./gradlew run` on the host).
    profiles: ["full"]
    depends_on:
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy
    environment:
      # Reach Postgres over the compose network by service name, not localhost.
      DATABASE_URL: jdbc:postgresql://postgres:5432/agentbrainviz
      POSTGRES_USER: agentviz
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: agentviz
      # Optional. Passed through from the host shell or the project's .env if set; otherwise blank,
      # and AI summarization is simply unavailable.
      AI_PROVIDER: ${AI_PROVIDER:-gemini}
      GEMINI_API_KEY: ${GEMINI_API_KEY:-}
      # Set this once the app is reachable off localhost, and pass the same value to agent-ingest.
      INGEST_TOKEN: ${INGEST_TOKEN:-}
      # Fail closed: with the container running -Ddotenv.enabled=false, the app only sees env vars
      # passed here (not a .env), so this must be forwarded explicitly for the flag to take effect.
      INGEST_REQUIRE_AUTH: ${INGEST_REQUIRE_AUTH:-}
      # Structured JSON logs by default in the container (one object per line, for a log aggregator).
      # Override with LOG_APPENDER=CONSOLE_TEXT for human-readable container logs.
      LOG_APPENDER: ${LOG_APPENDER:-CONSOLE_JSON}
    ports:
      - "8080:8080"
    healthcheck:
      # No curl in the JRE image, so use bash's /dev/tcp to make a minimal HTTP request to the
      # liveness probe and confirm a 200. Liveness (not readiness) on purpose: the app serves the UI
      # even when the store is down, so a database blip must not mark the container unhealthy.
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "bash -c 'exec 3<>/dev/tcp/localhost/8080 && printf \"GET /health HTTP/1.0\\r\\n\\r\\n\" >&3 && grep -q \"200 OK\" <&3'"]
      interval: 10s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 12
      start_period: 40s

volumes:
  pgdata:
